The Dark Side of the Boom (Goldman Sachs desk)
The Dark Side of the Boom (Citadel Securities' Nohshad Shah)

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: JPMorgan: the real oil risk moved 2,000 miles north of Hormuz
items.idea: JPMorgan's Natasha Kaneva argues the critical question is no longer whether crude can transit Hormuz, but whether the global refining system can process it once it returns. Russia accounts for roughly 20% of this year's decline in global refinery runs, and damage to hydrocrackers and catalytic crackers can keep capacity offline for quarters, not weeks — meaning ample crude supply could coexist with a persistent diesel/products shortage.
items.angle: Decouples 'oil up' from 'cracks up' — the real trade may be refining margins and refiners with spare complex capacity, not upstream crude exposure.
items.tweetA: JPMorgan's real oil call isn't about Hormuz anymore, it's about whether the world has enough working refineries. Russia alone is ~20% of this year's global refinery-run decline, and cracker damage takes quarters to fix. You can have abundant crude and a diesel shortage at the same time. $VLO $XLE
items.tweetB: The oil trade just split in two: crude supply and refining capacity are no longer the same bet. If Hormuz reopens tomorrow, crude eases, but Russian hydrocrackers don't come back online for quarters. That's a crack-spread trade, not a Brent trade.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: refinery-run decline and crack-spread data referenced in JPMorgan's Oil Markets Weekly.

items.title: War as the last lever before midterms
items.idea: MacroEdge's Don Johnson argues the administration wants nominal asset prices and inflation to run hot into the midterms as the last available lever to mask a softening bottom-80% economy, and that a prolonged Iran conflict (Trump signaled 60+ more days) serves that purpose by keeping energy and nominal prices elevated. Brent is up $14/bbl from the MOU lows amid a fourth consecutive night of US strikes and IRGC retaliation in Bahrain and Kuwait.
items.angle: Reframes the war-inflation link as a political utility function rather than a pure supply shock — implies less urgency to de-escalate than markets assume.
items.tweetA: MacroEdge's read on the Iran war: it's not a bug for Washington, it's a feature. The nominal wealth effect is the only lever left to mask a slowing bottom-80% economy before midterms, and Brent up $14 from the lows does exactly that. This isn't a war being fought against inflation, it's inflation being managed for optics.
items.tweetB: Real question nobody's asking: what happens when the war stops being 'inflationary but containable' and oil actually breaks $105? MacroEdge says that's the line where the political calculus flips from useful to disastrous. Fourth straight night of strikes says we're not moving away from that line.

charts.desc: 🔥 Brent rebound chart from MOU lows, +$14/bbl move highlighted in the note.

items.title: Asia is running the same wealth-effect playbook as the US — with worse leverage
items.idea: Johnson flags that South Korea and Japan are now as reliant on pumping equity markets to offset weak currencies as the US is, calling the combination of central bank stress, weak currencies, rising yields and speculative leverage 'the worst speculation in Asian market history.' He expects volatility to keep building through 2H rather than resolve.
items.angle: A structural setup for an Asia-driven volatility event most US-centric desks aren't pricing — worth tracking KOSPI/Nikkei leverage alongside the separate Topdown Charts call on Korean equity risk (item below).
items.tweetA: MacroEdge's Asia call: Korea and Japan are running the same 'pump the market to offset a weak currency' playbook as the US, except with worse leverage stacked on top. If volatility imports from anywhere in 2H, it's Seoul and Tokyo, not Washington.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Fed for the next vol shock. The actual fault line might be Asian currencies, leverage, and central banks all stressed at once — a combination one macro desk is calling the worst speculative setup in the region's market history.

items.title: Software flipped from a valuation premium to a discount vs. tech — almost overnight
items.idea: Software stocks have gone from trading at a major premium to a material discount versus the rest of tech, as the market prices software as a 'sunset industry' in the AI age. Callum Thomas argues that's an extreme worth fading: if AI is genuinely useful, incumbents with existing distribution and customer bases can deploy it just as easily as any new entrant, and barriers to entry from brand/distribution remain intact against 'vibe-coding' startups.
items.angle: A contrarian valuation-reset thesis — the 'AI kills software' narrative appears to have been priced in faster than the 'incumbents use AI too' counter-narrative.
items.tweetA: Software stocks: premium-to-tech to discount-to-tech, almost overnight, on the theory that AI kills the category. Nobody's asking why the incumbents with the distribution and the install base can't just deploy the same AI. That gap is the trade.
items.tweetB: If vibe-coding startups could actually out-compete software incumbents on distribution and brand, we'd have seen it by now. The valuation discount says the market already believes they can. The product roadmaps say otherwise.

charts.desc: 🔥 Software-sector relative-valuation chart showing the swing from premium to discount vs. broader tech — the core visual of the note.

items.title: Korean equities: a near-vertical run about to meet rate hikes
items.idea: Thomas flags Korean equities as at risk after a near-vertical run into extreme valuations, right as policy pivots toward rate hikes and an initial technical breakdown has begun.
items.angle: Direct read-through to the SK Hynix ADR mania flagged separately by Bloomberg — a valuation call and a retail-mania price move converging on the same overheated setup in the same week.
items.tweetA: Topdown Charts flags Korean equities at risk right as SK Hynix ADRs are up 27% in a session and the BOK pivots toward hikes. Two different newsletters, same week, converging on the same overheated setup.
items.tweetB: Vertical run, extreme valuation, rate-hike pivot, technical breakdown already started. That's not a stock-picking problem for Korea, that's a market-structure problem. Position sizing over conviction here.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (linked weekly report covers Korean equities, agri commodities, value vs. growth).

items.title: 60% of work hours are automatable — and almost nobody has the P&L to show for it
items.idea: McKinsey's Christian Jansen and co-authors argue advances in agentic and physical AI make close to 60% of work hours theoretically automatable, yet few companies have achieved meaningful P&L impact because they bolt AI onto existing processes instead of redesigning the operating model into what they call a 'symbiotic enterprise.'
items.angle: The institutional-consulting version of the AI productivity paradox — useful ammunition against the bull case that capex converts linearly into earnings.
items.tweetA: McKinsey says 60% of work hours are theoretically automatable with agentic AI right now. Also McKinsey: almost nobody has the P&L to show for it. The gap isn't the model, it's that companies keep bolting AI onto old org charts instead of redesigning them.
items.tweetB: $750B+ in hyperscaler capex this year, and the actual constraint on ROI isn't compute or model quality, it's whether anyone bothers to redesign how work gets done. That's a much harder problem than buying more GPUs, and scaling capex doesn't solve it.

items.title: What Messi and Ronaldo's 6th World Cup says about executive performance
items.idea: McKinsey draws a direct parallel between elite athletes extending careers into their late 30s via sports-science investment (personalized recovery, data, nutrition) and what its research says CEOs need to do to sustain performance: disciplined time management, prioritized recovery, continuous learning, reliance on data.
items.angle: A soft signal that the 'optimization economy' longevity mindset has moved from Silicon Valley biohackers into mainstream management frameworks — with second-order relevance for corporate health/performance vendors.
items.tweetA: McKinsey is now telling CEOs to train like Messi and Ronaldo — data-driven recovery, personalized programs, treating your 50s like an athlete treats their late 30s. The longevity-optimization mindset has fully jumped from biohackers to boardrooms.
items.tweetB: When a management consultancy uses professional athletes as the leadership benchmark, that's a tell the market for executive talent has gotten as competitive and data-driven as elite sports. Worth watching what that does to demand for corporate performance/health vendors.

items.title: Nearly 1 in 5 luxury shoppers now buy resale — and AI is accelerating it
items.idea: McKinsey's State of the Consumer report finds resale moving to the center of luxury retail: nearly one in five surveyed luxury shoppers purchased through resale platforms in the past six months, led by younger consumers seeking provenance and craftsmanship rather than discounts. AI is accelerating listings, discovery and pricing, though human verification remains essential for authenticity trust.
items.angle: A structural read on luxury demand shifting toward resale rather than new purchases — relevant to how you think about luxury-goods multiples and resale-platform positioning.
items.tweetA: 1 in 5 luxury shoppers bought resale in the last 6 months, and it's not the bargain-hunters, it's younger buyers hunting provenance and pieces you can't get new. That's a demand-structure shift for luxury multiples, not a recession signal.
items.tweetB: AI is automating resale listings and pricing, but McKinsey notes trust still requires a human in the loop for authentication. That's the actual moat for resale platforms right now, not the AI tooling everyone assumes will commoditize the category.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Goldman Sachs desk)
items.title: Goldman's oil forecast is still $80. Its tail risk just moved to $110.
items.idea: Goldman holds a base case of $80 Brent for Q4 2026 and $75 for 2027, but its distribution has widened sharply: Persian Gulf export flows fell from above 80% of normal (post-MOU) back below 50% after renewed tanker attacks, doubling the estimated net loss of Gulf flows to 13.4 million barrels/day. China's collapse in crude imports (down ~5mbpd) has cushioned the disruption but may be nearing its limit.
items.angle: The headline number is stale — the real signal is a bank widening its tail risk to $110 while holding the center forecast flat, which is how sell-side desks quietly reprice fear without changing the number clients see in the summary line.
items.tweetA: Goldman's headline number is still $80 Brent. The number buried in the note is $110 tail risk, because Gulf export flows just collapsed from 80% of normal back below 50%. When a bank widens the tails and keeps the center flat, that's the real repricing.
items.tweetB: 13.4 million barrels/day of net Gulf flow loss, double the prior estimate, and the offset has been China simply buying less oil. That's not a stable equilibrium, that's a China import bounce waiting to happen the moment prices look cheap enough.

charts.desc: 🔥 Persian Gulf export-flow recovery/collapse chart (80% of normal down to below 50% post-MOU).

items.title: DeepSeek is back in the market for its second $7B-scale round in a month
items.idea: Per the FT via Michael Parekh, DeepSeek is in preliminary talks to raise new funds just one month after closing $7B at a $52B valuation, reportedly targeting a $71B pre-money valuation (a 37% jump), driven by data-center buildout and expansion from chatbot to autonomous agents. China's state-backed Integrated Circuit Industry Investment Fund is already a minority shareholder alongside CATL and Tencent.
items.angle: The pace of Chinese AI fundraising is now matching or outpacing US labs — a one-month gap between mega-rounds is a Silicon Valley cadence, not the historical rhythm of Chinese AI funding.
items.tweetA: DeepSeek raised $7B a month ago. It's already back in the market talking about a 37% valuation bump to $71B. That's not Chinese AI catching up to Silicon Valley funding speed, that's Chinese AI setting the pace.
items.tweetB: A state-backed semiconductor fund is already a DeepSeek shareholder, CATL and Tencent are in the cap table, and the new money is going straight into data centers and agent infrastructure. This isn't a chatbot startup raising a round, it's a national compute strategy wearing a startup's cap table.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Soft CPI didn't fight inflation, it bought the Fed time
items.idea: June CPI fell 0.4% MoM (vs. -0.1% expected) with core flat versus +0.2% expected — the broadest disinflation print in months, driven partly by a 9.7% gasoline drop but broad enough (education, apparel, used cars, medical care all down) to be credible. 2yr yields fell ~10bp, 10yr ~6bp, and priced July hike odds fell below 4bp.
items.angle: The framing that matters is 'kicked the can,' not 'inflation defeated' — shelter (35% of CPI) is the real disinflation engine and it's structural, so it can keep grinding lower even as oil stays elevated from Hormuz.
items.tweetA: Core CPI printed -0.017%, to three decimals. That's not a rounding error, that's the Fed getting bailed out by shelter, 35% of the basket, while everyone stares at the 9.7% gasoline drop. Watch rents, not oil, for where this goes next.
items.tweetB: The Fed didn't win anything here. It got a one-month reprieve that lets Warsh keep hawkish language intact for Congress while privately not hiking. That's a very different trade than 'inflation is over,' and the oil desk down the hall knows it.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: CPI component-breadth breakdown and rates-market reaction.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: PJM just failed its power auction for the third year running — 6.8GW short
items.idea: PJM Interconnection's capacity auction for the year beginning June 2028 came up 6,831 MW short of its reliability requirement, worse than the prior auction's ~6,500 MW shortfall. Capacity cleared at the regulatory price ceiling of $325/MW-day; uncapped, PJM estimates most of the grid would have cleared near $554.72, and ComEd near $776.69 — a price signal roughly 70% higher than what regulators let consumers see.
items.angle: The concrete number behind 'AI hits the power wall.' A price ceiling is suppressing the market signal that's supposed to summon new generation, which means the shortfall likely worsens before it improves, and the bottleneck economically favors turbine/transformer/nuclear suppliers over hyperscalers themselves.
items.tweetA: PJM's power auction cleared at a $325/MW-day price ceiling. Uncapped, it would've been $554-$777. Regulators are hiding ~70% of the real scarcity price from consumers, which means the market signal meant to build new power plants isn't getting through. The AI power wall just got a price tag, and it's being censored. $VST $CEG
items.tweetB: 6.8 gigawatts short, for the third auction in a row, in the region that houses Data Center Alley. Capital for data centers moves in weeks. Transmission lines move at the speed of public hearings. That mismatch is the actual bottleneck in the AI trade now, not GPU supply.

charts.desc: 🔥 PJM capacity-auction clearing price vs. uncapped estimate ($325 vs. $554-777/MW-day).

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Industrials are up 30% this year and still one of the least-owned sectors on Wall Street
items.idea: S&P 500 industrials tied to the AI power/data-center buildout are up roughly 30% year-to-date, several trading above 35x forward earnings, with electrical-equipment forward margins at 17.8% (Yardeni), more than double financial-crisis-era levels. Yet investor positioning in industrials reportedly remains near multi-year lows.
items.angle: A rare 'expensive and under-owned' combination — valuation says the picks-and-shovels trade has been discovered, positioning data says it hasn't been crowded into yet, which is unusual and worth tracking as a possible re-rating catalyst.
items.tweetA: Industrials up 30% this year, trading at 35x forward earnings, margins double their financial-crisis level, and investor positioning is still near multi-year lows. Expensive and unloved at the same time almost never happens. Something has to give. $ETN $GEV
items.tweetB: The picks-and-shovels AI trade stopped being cheap a while ago. The market has priced turbine and switchgear makers like software companies. The only thing that hasn't caught up is fund positioning, which means the re-rating risk is still ahead of most portfolios, not behind them.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: S&P 500 industrials/electrical-equipment forward margin and valuation trend.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom
items.title: Washington is quietly building oil pipelines around Iran's chokepoint
items.idea: The US is backing Iraq-Syria efforts to rehabilitate the Kirkuk-Baniyas pipeline to move Iraqi crude to the Mediterranean, bypassing the Gulf entirely, while DP World expands Fujairah port capacity and Abu Dhabi accelerates a second pipeline to double export capacity through Fujairah by 2027 — permanent infrastructure responses to what is nominally a temporary blockade.
items.angle: Even if the current Hormuz crisis resolves, the strategic bypass infrastructure doesn't get un-built — a multi-year bearish development for Iran's geopolitical leverage over oil, independent of near-term price action.
items.tweetA: Wars end. Pipelines don't get un-built. Washington backing the Kirkuk-Baniyas revival and the UAE doubling Fujairah's export capacity by 2027 means Iran's Hormuz leverage keeps shrinking even after this crisis ends. That's a structural short on Iran's option value, not a trade on this week's tanker count.
items.tweetB: Every barrel that gets a Mediterranean or Gulf-of-Oman exit is a barrel Iran can't threaten anymore. The market's pricing this week's blockade. It should be pricing the fact that the region is now building permanent insurance against the next one.

items.title: 1-year inflation swaps just dropped below 2% for the first time since the 2024 election
items.idea: Authers highlights that 1-year inflation swaps fell below the Fed's 2% target for the first time since the 2024 election after the soft June CPI, alongside the second-largest single-day drop in next-meeting Fed pricing since 2008 (Deutsche Bank). Yet oil dipped only briefly before bouncing back on Hormuz risk, with Trump's proposed 20% cargo fee reversed within 24 hours.
items.angle: The swaps market is pricing a dovish outcome that assumes energy stays contained, but Hormuz-driven oil strength directly undermines the disinflation thesis those swaps are betting on.
items.tweetA: 1-year inflation swaps just went below 2% for the first time since the 2024 election. Same week Brent is holding near $78 on an active Hormuz blockade. One of these markets is wrong about what July's oil price does to August's CPI.
items.tweetB: Second-largest single-day repricing of Fed odds since 2008, on one CPI print, in a month where the Fed chair explicitly refused to say 'mission accomplished.' That's not conviction, that's a crowded trade in the rates market looking for any excuse to run.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: inflation-swaps-vs-target chart and CME hike-odds swing.

items.title: Goldman just posted the biggest trading quarter in Wall Street history — from an oil war and an AI panic
items.idea: JPMorgan generated a record $6B from equity trading; Goldman posted $7.42B, the highest quarterly trading haul ever recorded by a Wall Street bank — both driven by volatility from the AI selloff and the Iran war. The KBW Bank Index's price-to-book multiple is now at a post-GFC high. Dimon: 'it's getting close to as good as it gets.'
items.angle: Record bank trading revenue during a war and an AI panic signals that dislocation, not calm, is now the banks' most profitable environment — worth reframing how 'good' bank earnings get read.
items.tweetA: Goldman just posted the single biggest trading quarter in Wall Street history, $7.42B, and it happened because of an oil war and an AI panic, not because markets were calm. When Dimon says 'as good as it gets,' he's telling you the volatility is the business model now, not a headwind to it. $GS $JPM
items.tweetB: Bank of America wants investors rotating out of chip stocks to land in bank stocks as 'an island of stability.' But that stability is priced off trading desks harvesting record profit from the exact instability everyone's fleeing. That's not diversification, that's the same volatility wearing a different ticker.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: KBW Bank Index price-to-book at post-GFC high.

items.title: IBM's worst day ever, SK Hynix's best — the AI dispersion trade in one session
items.idea: IBM posted its first-ever single-day fall of more than 25% on disappointing results, the same day SK Hynix's newly launched ADRs gained 27.2%, roughly double IBM's drop in magnitude. SK Hynix's Korean market cap fell $504B over three weeks before regaining $56B in a day, and remains $603B above its January 1 level.
items.angle: The AI trade isn't a single 'winner' story anymore, it's a dispersion machine — a 25% one-day loss and a 27% one-day gain in AI-adjacent names on the same day is a volatility regime, not a bull or bear market.
items.tweetA: IBM's worst day in company history and SK Hynix's ADR debut popping 27% happened on the SAME day. Nobody can tell you if AI names are a buy or a sell right now because the honest answer is both, simultaneously, depending on which part of the stack you own.
items.tweetB: SK Hynix lost $504B in Korean market cap in three weeks, then added $56B back in a single session, and is STILL up $603B since January. Levered ETFs now let retail trade this swing directly. That's not investing in memory chips, that's gambling on volatility with extra steps.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening: IBM daily-return chart and SK Hynix market-cap swing.

items.title: AI IPOs are now functioning as FOMC events for markets
items.idea: Capital Flows argues AI IPOs (Cerebras marked the top in NVIDIA, SK Hynix marked the low, almost to the day) act like liquidity-signaling events comparable to Fed meetings, and that the AI trade has splintered into four distinct trades (chipmakers, hyperscalers, power, China) that most portfolios still hedge as a single trade, with the binding constraint migrating from wafers to memory to power.
items.angle: If most books are still hedging 'AI' as one trade while the market has already split it into four, that's a structural mispricing worth auditing your own hedge structure against.
items.tweetA: Capital Flows' framework: AI IPOs are the new FOMC events for liquidity signals — Cerebras topped NVIDIA, SK Hynix bottomed it, almost to the day. If that's right, watch the IPO calendar the way you watch the Fed calendar.
items.tweetB: The AI trade split into four separate trades, chips, hyperscalers, power, China, months ago. Most portfolios are still hedging it like it's one trade. The binding constraint keeps migrating down the chain, wafers to memory to power, and a single-basket hedge can't follow a moving bottleneck.

items.title: Trump's 20% Hormuz cargo fee dies within 24 hours of the Strait staying effectively shut
items.idea: The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively shut after Tehran's closure declaration and CENTCOM's fourth strike wave (roughly a fifth of world seaborne oil normally transits it); Trump proposed a 20% cargo fee on ships receiving US assistance transiting Hormuz, then reversed course within 24 hours. Brent held $77-79.
items.angle: A policy proposal killed within a day is itself a data point — it signals the White House is testing pain thresholds on shipping/oil markets in real time, meaning Hormuz headline risk will keep whipsawing faster than positioning can adjust.
items.tweetA: Trump floated a 20% cargo fee on ships through Hormuz and killed it within 24 hours. That's not policy, that's a live pain-threshold test on oil markets. Expect more one-day reversals like this, position for whipsaw, not direction.
items.tweetB: A fifth of the world's seaborne oil normally transits a Strait that's 'effectively shut' for the fourth strike wave running, and Brent is sitting at $77-79 like it's a Tuesday. Either the market is dangerously complacent about supply risk, or everyone's already pricing a resolution nobody's actually negotiating.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (full research link in email).

items.title: BTC broke its 200-week moving average — the level that called every prior bear market
items.idea: Bitcoin rallied nearly 5% to $64,830 on the soft CPI print, squeezing $134M in shorts within an hour, but remains below its 200-week moving average, the level that has flagged the start of every prior bear market — meaning today's bounce is a relief rally inside a technically damaged structure, not a repaired one. Ethereum outperformed, up 5.4-6% vs. BTC's 2.7-3%, with Bitmine now holding 4.8% of ETH supply.
items.angle: The CPI-driven crypto rally is a macro trade, not a crypto-fundamentals trade — the structural signal (the 200-week MA break) hasn't been resolved by one good inflation print, arguing for banking gains into strength rather than treating this as a trend reversal.
items.tweetA: BTC ripped 5% on soft CPI and squeezed $134M of shorts in an hour. It's still below its 200-week moving average, the exact level that's called every prior bear market start. A great macro day doesn't repair a broken technical structure. Bank the bounce, don't marry it.
items.tweetB: The real crypto story this week isn't the CPI bounce, it's that ETH is outperforming BTC by roughly 2x on the move (Bitmine now owns 4.8% of ETH supply) while BTC is the one still under its bear-market line. If you have to pick a major right now, the market's already telling you which one.

charts.desc: 🔥 BTC price vs. 200-week moving average chart.

items.title: The CLARITY Act has one shot before August 7, or crypto regulation slides to 2027
items.idea: A new CLARITY Act draft may drop this week, with the August 7 Senate recess as the last realistic legislative gate (Galaxy pegs it at 50-50 odds); institutional allocators are reportedly 'actively exploring digital-asset exposure but withholding capital' pending defined regulatory guidance, meaning real sidelined capital is gated on this single date.
items.angle: A concrete, dated catalyst most macro-only readers won't be tracking — if it slips, that's a specific, nameable reason institutional crypto flows stay soft through year-end regardless of price action.
items.tweetA: August 7 is the date crypto bulls should have circled, not the next CPI print. If the CLARITY Act doesn't clear the Senate before the recess, US market-structure regulation slides to 2027, and the institutional capital that's 'exploring' digital assets stays exactly where it is: on the sidelines.
items.tweetB: 50-50 odds, per Galaxy, on a single legislative date deciding whether institutional crypto demand shows up this cycle or waits another year. That's a binary, dated, mostly-ignored catalyst sitting right in the middle of an otherwise noisy macro tape.

items.source: The Dark Side of the Boom (Citadel Securities' Nohshad Shah)
items.title: 'Where is the good news?' — Citadel's EMEA fixed income head sees three bears chasing Goldilocks
items.idea: Citadel Securities' Nohshad Shah frames the market's Goldilocks narrative (resilient growth, falling inflation, uninterrupted AI capex) as under threat from three simultaneous forces: renewed geopolitical escalation, a tightening bias in financial conditions, and growing cracks beneath the AI investment story.
items.angle: A senior sell-side voice explicitly asking 'where is the good news' during a week the market is celebrating a soft CPI print is a useful contrarian counterweight against the more bullish framing elsewhere in the same news cycle.
items.tweetA: Citadel's own EMEA fixed income head is asking 'where is the good news' in the same week markets are celebrating a soft CPI. Geopolitical escalation, tightening financial conditions, and AI-capex cracks don't go away because one inflation print looked clean.
items.tweetB: Everyone's trading the Goldilocks narrative this week. The desk that actually prices flow risk for a living is naming three separate bears circling it. Worth more than a headline CPI beat.

items.title: Don't trade the mirage: June's soft CPI describes a world that already stopped existing
items.idea: Princeton Chen's framework: June's CPI cooled almost entirely because gasoline fell ~10% during a ceasefire that collapsed July 8, meaning the data describes conditions that no longer exist. Core CPI is still ~2.85% and sticky, shelter ~3.4%, the Fed is reportedly split 9-8 on hiking, and September hike odds sit near 70%.
items.angle: Directly corroborates, independently, the same 'backward-looking CPI' read found in the Bloomberg, MacroEdge, and Princeton News Wall items above — three separate sources converging on the same stale-gasoline mechanism is a stronger signal than any one alone.
items.tweetA: Three different newsletters this morning, three different angles, same core catch: June's soft CPI ran on a gasoline drop from a ceasefire that died July 8. When that many independent reads converge on the same stale-data problem, it's not noise, it's the actual trade.
items.tweetB: Fed split 9-8 on hiking, core sticky at 2.85%, shelter still 3.4%, and July's oil-driven CPI print is already baked to look worse. The 'inflation is over' relief rally in tech is exactly the kind of move this framework says to fade, not chase.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (full research PDF linked in email).
